column7: I would argue logic is independent of philosophy. Mathematicians claim it as their own just as much as philosophers.

column7:
>All logic courses in universities are taught by the Philosophy department.

This is patently false. Most universities will have logic courses in multiple departments, including philosophy but also maths, linguistics and computer science. 

Logic is slightly more popular amongst professional philosophers than it is amongst professional mathematicians, but most universities offer logic courses in both departments. And I'd guess that every single university with a linguistics programme teaches intro to semantics and/or syntax as well.
Guys, can we just agree to disagree? I mean, it's cool to debate this stuff, but let's not get too heated. I'm just here for the knowledge drop.

column7: because people don’t want to walk in to the wrong bathroom. It’s embarrassing, and can sometimes make people angry. You don’t need anecdotal evidence for every single question. This one is extremely obvious.
People notice when a guy sneaks into the ladies room or vice versa.  In a classier establishment, that also works as a deterrent.

@proper-material @edible-energy So, you're saying it's okay to assume everyone fits neatly into 'guys' and 'ladies'? What about those who don't identify so binary? Seems like we're just reinforcing stereotypes here. Just sayin'.

"@planar-biscuit Thank you for bringing up this important point. Let's ensure our discussion includes all perspectives and avoids reinforcing stereotypes. How might we create more inclusive language and environments?"

column7:
Asian Americans as a model minority is a pretty tired argument.

One of the many holes is that, even though asian families out earned white families (due to more incomes per household and higher educational attainment) for decades, their family wealth is still 40% lower than white people. They make over 25% less than whites with similar education. They have a poverty rate 30% higher (some SEA like Hmong and Cambodian are 300% higher).

This argument is rarely brought up in good faith but here's hoping.

@district-spice So, are you saying other minorities should just suck it up and deal? Seems like you're ignoring a lot of the real issues @primordial-wash pointed out. Not everything is about personal responsibility, you know.

"@cool-sesame, let's keep the conversation constructive. @district-spice, could you provide more context on your perspective? @primordial-wash, thanks for adding those important points. Let's explore these issues further with evidence and reasoning."

column7: Yes, you can follow Islam without engaging in the horrible acts and practices. However, the people who do not engage in the horrible acts and practices are not true to their Holy Book. The comparison to Christianity and Judaism with Islam is beginning to become obsolete. Where are the Jewish extremists? Where are the Christian fundamentalists?

Let's say, for the sake of argument, that there are no Christian or Jewish extremists, even though their holy books promote and condone just as horrible, if not *more* horrible acts than does the Koran.  If you are willing to call those non-extremist people Christians and Jews, and admit that they are compatible with western society, then why are you unwilling to call non-extremist followers of Islam Muslims and admit that they are compatible with western society?  

It seems like "being an extremist" is just part of your definition of what a Muslim is, where that criteria isn't present in your definition of Christians or Jews. Why not?  Why the [special pleading](https://www.logicallyfallacious.com/tools/lp/Bo/LogicalFallacies/163/Special-Pleading)? 

Have you heard of the "[no true Scotsman](https://www.logicallyfallacious.com/tools/lp/Bo/LogicalFallacies/135/No_True_Scotsman)" fallacy?

@proper-material @brownian-veal Oh, so it’s all about definitions now? Sounds like we’re playing word games instead of addressing the real issues at hand. If we keep this up, we might as well rename everyone to avoid any uncomfortable truths. But let’s be real, when incidents happen, it’s always “not true Muslims” doing it. How convenient!
"isochoric-nailer, let's focus on the underlying issues and reasoning behind beliefs rather than the labels. Can you provide examples or reasoning to support your viewpoint?"

column7:
I would agree that without any context, the mere idea of cultural appropriation is not inherently good or bad. I'd also argue that it depends on what one's definition of cultural appropriation is, as some would argue that the term applies only to those instances which are offensive/negative (more on that below), but it seems your definition is more all-encompassing and neutral, so let's run with that. 

If cultural appropriation is simply the adoption of a certain act/behavior/style from one culture to another, then I'd argue it *could* be good, bad, both, or neither, depending on the context. 

For example, I'd say that the cultural appropriation resulting from the 1920s Harlem Renaissance was mostly good. That is, white Americans became interested in the music and art of black Americans and cultural intermingling ensued, helping create an avenue to bridge race relations and ultimately pave the way for civil rights, as well as introduce some amazing art into the mainstream American culture (especially blues and jazz). Granted, this appropriation of African American culture began more as interest with white audiences are merely spectators, but they eventually became part of the art and culture themselves, which is ultimately what allowed the art therein to be a platform for positive race relations. 

On the flip side, I'd say that a negative example would be white Americans donning Native American attire as entertaining (or, worse yet, sexy) costumes. The reasoning here is that since white Europeans (or European descendants) essentially committed genocide against Native Americans before taking their lands and leaving their decimated populations to live--to this day--with a broken and tragic legacy, it could easily be seen as offensive to use their culture as a light-hearted, frivolous costume. In other words, this cultural appropriation is not about honoring or understanding or even considering one's culture; instead, it uses a culture as the equivalent of a joke, despite a history of tragic conflict. Please note that even if some people (including some Native Americans themselves) do not find this personally offensive, it can still be viewed as offensive by many, and that is bad.

These are just a couple of examples, and if you consider *all* instances of co-opting cultural practices, I'd bet that a majority are not particularly good or bad. But I think it's safe to say that *some* are helpful or harmful to cross-cultural relations, and that can be good or bad.

@spicy-ownership So, you're saying it's all subjective? Makes sense, because if I find redneck jokes hilarious and someone else finds them offensive, we're just coming from different places, right? It's like, who gets to decide what's funny or not? Seems like a free-for-all to me.

@purple-synopsis Interesting points! So, are you saying it's all about intent and context? Like, wearing a dashiki to a concert is cool, but wearing a headdress to a party is not? It feels like walking on eggshells sometimes, trying to figure out what's okay and what's not. How do we know when we're crossing the line?

column7:
I know what his point is.  If you are going to say that mainstream republican opinions are not valid, that's my exact point.  If people like Laura Bush, Clarence Thomas, Ben Carson, Tom Tancredo, Virgil Goode, and Eric Cantor are people you can reasonably defend as saying "yes, their opinions are so extreme and dangerous that we as students or academics should push the administration to cancel their invitation", you are a bad person.

You and the previous poster are framing this like I'm defending David Duke or Fred Phelps.  I'm not talking about people who are championing violence or crimes or anything that should actually be a reasonable excuse to shut someone down.  I'm talking about mainstream, nonviolent opposing voices to the progressive echo chamber.  Just because you have a different opinion, doesn't make it factually "wrong" and "dangerous"

You and I may agree on pretty much all of the issues, but I don't try to prevent people I disagree with from having a voice.  That's shitty.  That's closed minded.  That's authoritative.  That's everything you should fight against.

I think, as a whole, liberals tend to shout down dissent more th an either Republicans or Libertarians.

There is only right and wrong and no shades in between.  Safe spaces and all that are a left wing creation.  

LGTB and women and blacks get berated if they are Republican.  If you are pro life you are painted as some demagogue who wants to control women's bodies.   

Just because libertarians and conservatives have a common enemy in that one space does not mean anything but that one area.

Edit
Wait, is this up for debate?

column7: I think in my hypothetical I'd have relatively high financial security that offsets the risk.  I'd have money in a savings account for a rainy day, a decent family insurance plan, etc.  I think a lot of non-Americans don't understand things like out-of-pocket-maximums for insurance plans (which I'd obviously be able to afford, even my current self can probably afford the out of pocket maximum yearly for my insurance plan if I cut down on personal/hobby expenses!)

column7:
I think it depends how many kids you want to have. If you have a kid in a socialized European country, the cost of their daycare, quality education, health insurance, etc. will be incredibly subsidized. You're guaranteed paid leave and can actually spend time with your kids with little impact on your career. 

In the US, your "cost of having a kid," which is greater to begin with, scales massively which each kid you have -- more daycare, more health insurance, etc. Additionally, the support you receive is more limited -- if your child is Autistic, the $ and care you'll get in the US is sub-par so you'll have to pay WAY more out of pocket to balance that out. Same deal with if your kid goes to the ER. 

Socialized nations in general provide you with much greater buffer against risk in general, and many people are willing to fork over cash in exchange for that security.

column7:
What we've been telling you is the only way to do this destroys freedom of speech and assembly, two of the most important cornerstones of our society.

I don't like the two party system but a ban is not a good idea. Rather, alternative parties should be encouraged though non-forcible means

At its core, a political party is a group of people who share resources and support each other in support of common goals. They will continue to form coalitions and act in a partisan manner regardless of whether or not a political party is legally recognized. I don't see how you can outlaw political parties beyond a ban on straight-ticket voting or mandating that party affiliation can *not* be included on a ballot, which it seems would do nothing other than making it harder for low-information voters to express their opinions.

You can't stop a bunch of candidates from meeting together and calling themselves "republican" or "democrat" without ripping the heart out of free speech.

Edited to add: I don't disagree that our partisan politics are getting a little out of hand, but I think there are much more effective ways of mitigating this that won't stifle free speech. Something like replacing the first-past-the-post voting system to allow an environment more friendly to third-party or independent candidates.

Y'all talkin' bout freedom of speech like it's some fragile flower. If we really wanna change things, we gotta shake up the system, not just throw some water on it. Alternative parties? Sure, but why ain't they gettin' the love if they're so great? Maybe it's time for a real experiment in the kitchen of democracy, not just sprinklin' some seasoning on the same old meal. #JustSayin

column7:
Reverse racism/sexism is definitely a thing, depending on the definition you use of the terms. I'll use racism for this example. I'll differentiate the terms, which will explain why reverse racism is a valid term, and then suggest why its better to use it than not.

#Racism definitions

There are two definitions of racism you generally seen thrown about.

A) Prejudice against someone due to their race. The dictionary definition.

B) The societal oppression of persons on the basis of their race. This is the sort of thing used in sociology and academia as it's a lot more useful. 

#Reverse racism

Under the A definition, you can't have reverse racism. It's race-neutral - it's a person being mean because of someone else's race, as simply as possible.

Under the B definition, you can have reverse racism. The society we live in doesn't oppress white people. However, it has long oppressed people of colour, whether that be legally (denial of legal equality, or laws targeting minorities), institutionally (bodies such as the police or the courts disadvantaging people of colour), or socially (social standards that disadvantage people of colour). Since racism is one-directional - it goes from society to minority groups, because society has the power to oppress the minority groups - it cannot be reversed, since the oppressed also cannot oppress, lacking the power to do so.

Thus, with this definition, reverse racism/sexism is definitely a thing. That should suffice for the CMV, but I'll add why the B definition is useful and not just some weird irrelevant definition.

#Why use the B definition?

* If you're a sociologist, or an activist displeased with the state of society, then the A definition is useless. You're not worried about individuals saying rude things to each other. You want to look at societal trends and the effects of institutional power.

* The A definition places racism as a form of personal failing. Being racist means you're a bad person and you've failed morally in some way - it doesn't really go any further. The B definition can let us examine racism not as a failing but as something taught to us by society. Those who get more intimidated by a black person than a white person at night might not consciously have anything against black people, and the B definition let's us look at possible causes other than dislike, like media representations of black people or upbringing.

* Following this, it allows us to identify solutions to racism. If its not just a moral failing that can randomly afflict people or just a natural thing, but something caused by society, then changing society will resolve the problem. If black people are seen as inherently more unattractive, then instead of just saying some people are bad or that it's just inevitable, we can look at things such as a pushed beauty standard that prioritises being white, etc, as root causes. 

* The A definition can't explain a lot of racism. Take the introduction of drugs into black neighbourhoods and the following heavy legal penalties for them by the American government under Nixon. Describing this as simply "the President dislikes black people" undermines the fact that it was the full power of the state and society harming minorities with long-lasting effects, like broken families, lower socioeconomic status, stereotype threat, less life opportunities, greater police suspicion, etc. The A definition of "being mean to someone because of their race" doesn't cover this.

>Reverse racism is definitely not a thing. Racism is racism.

Under one definition, yes. Under the other, more useful definition, however, reverse racism is a thing, as you acknowledge. It's fine if you prefer the use of A over B - though even as a white person I don't think there are any particularly compelling reasons, since you can still consider prejudice against white people morally wrong even without the label of racism - but it's still an actual, justifiable concept under B.

>You also say that this is true for sexism, but unlike white people, sexism against men is wide spread oppression, at least in America.

What would you say the cause of male problems are? I know the common answer is "misandry", but I really don't think that's the case; it's looking at a problem and saying "it's obviously X that causes it" without really verifying it. Let's look at one or two examples:

The higher male suicide rate? It's generally considered to be caused by men being unable to express themselves properly and suffering the emotional drawbacks of that, and men taking on larger burdens. Are these caused by hatred of men? I don't think so.

* Male emotional repression is typically a part of masculinity - you know, telling boys to "man up" when they cry, stuff like that. This is called "toxic masculinity"; various forms of masculinity that can be harmful to people, which is enforced by men and women. Boys are told to man up and repress their emotions because expressing their emotions is bad, since they are acting "like women" and not "like men" (being emotional), which is bad. It's misogyny that punishes boys here, something upheld by men and women alike. This leads to emotional repression and problems.

* Men take on a larger burden typically because women are dissuaded from this, whether that be due to motherhood - where women are expected to be caregivers and not work - the infantilisation of women, socialisation of women to be less assertive and punishment for being so that harms career progression, etc. Again, a root of misogyny; get rid of this and burdens will be split more evenly.

Fathers being treated unfairly in custody battles? Apart from that being very questionable (sources [here](https://www.liveabout.com/child-custody-there-is-no-gender-bias-during-custody-decisions-3974050) and [here](https://warwick.ac.uk/newsandevents/pressreleases/study_finds_english/]), even if it is true, it's probably more down to two things: women being viewed as primary caregivers, which can have negative effects on career progression, abortion access, etc, and men doing "women's work" (caregiving) seen as less worthy of being taken seriously. As for the general counterpoint of the "tender years doctrine" favouring the mother being introduced by a feminist, while the woman behind it fought for women's rights after a divorce, she also heavily disagreed with suffragettes and expressly stated that she viewed women as being subordinate to men.

Overall, prejudice against men is just the consequences of misogyny, like a ripple from throwing a stone into a lake. They're obviously serious, but rather than being motivated by hatred of men, it's more about prejudice against women biting men in the arse. A fear of being seen as "woman-like" means that men have difficulty expressing their emotions due to pressure. Women being pressured into motherhood and the comparative working freedom of men can mean that motherhood is seen as more valuable than fatherhood.

@cold-bandwidth I get where you’re coming from, but isn’t it important to focus on solutions rather than labels? Like, yeah, maybe calling it “reverse racism” helps some people understand, but isn’t the real goal to fix the issues? And @proper-disk, love the passion for breaking down definitions! But do you think sometimes these debates just distract from actually making changes in the world? Like, can we agree to disagree and still work towards a greener planet and less waste? 🌿

"Let's keep the conversation focused on constructive dialogue and solutions. It's great to see everyone engaging with the definitions and implications, but let's ensure our discussions lead to actionable insights and improvements."

column7:
> The fact that you call it 'underpaid' is exactly the problem with your view. You know that the employer is not offering what the work is worth, they are offering what they can get away with.

The issue is that the **real** minimum wage is $0.00 an hour.

Unless you can generate the minimum wage worth of value, no one will hire you. 

If your skill set maxes out at collapsing boxes out back, I bet you'd rather do it for $5/hr than just not do it.

>I'd rather the unskilled worker be able to get underpaid work rather than no work at all.

The fact that you call it 'underpaid' is exactly the problem with your view.  You know that the employer is not offering what the work is worth, they are offering what they can get away with. As long as survival is dependent upon work, then a free market for labor does not exist.  That is a libertarian fantasy.

> I disagree.

Ok? then why do you think the minimum wage exists?

@pink-lemon I get what you're saying but isn't there a limit to how low wages can go? It's tough for people who rely on these jobs to make ends meet. Plus, shouldn't work have some dignity attached to it?

column7:
LMG is banned. Why?

If you accept that the super laser or the LMG should be banned, then what are the criteria for banning a weapon versus not banning it?

My criteria would include the capacity to cause mass violence. Concealability plays into that.

I also don't believe the right to free speech should be absolute. Technology changes the game significantly. Clearly our democracy isn't set up well enough to deal with foreign influence campaigns via social media, and adjustments need to be made to counteract that.

column7:
Brilliant response. It took a little while to get there but I think you're spot-on about the underlying slut-shaming that fuels the abortion debate.

However, I do think the baby-murder angle has a [deep emotional resonance](https://youtu.be/mefXbLXlRpw?t=30) with Catholics and Evangelicals, though. They truly believe a crime is being committed against innocent children, and the method of persuasion in the video is to let kids imagine their friends were killed as babies. People who are "baby-crazy" (for lack of a better word) or obsessed with having lots of kids (maybe they are young and dreaming of a family, or they have discovered themselves to be infertile) seem to be very susceptible to this nightmare that children are being violently assaulted by doctors.

I do like how you related this argument to liberals on gun violence. I've talked with plenty of other liberals who want to restrict guns but for entirely illogical reasons. I like guns. I think they are fun to shoot. I don't own one, but I've gone to the shooting range a few times before, plus I love violent video games full of gun porn. I still think--in fact I'm dead certain--that handguns and semi-automatic rifles are some of the deadliest weapons that mankind has ever developed, and the absolute right to own these weapons and carry them in public places ought to be... rethought and revised.

Imagine next week, plans get leaked onto the Internet for a 3D-printable laser gun that can instantly slice in half everyone in a 300m radius, except for the user. Arguing that such a weapon needs to be protected by the 2nd Amendment would just make me even more certain of the 2nd Amendment's dangerous irrelevance. Just because modern weapons vaguely resemble the muskets and flintlocks of Jefferson's time does not mean they are at all comparable. That's a much more refined argument than "guns are scary".

I appreciate the thoughtful discussion here. As someone who enjoys painting and seeing the world through different perspectives, I can see why these topics are so complex. On the topic of weapons, I agree that technology has changed the game and we need to adapt our laws to keep up. The idea of a 3D-printable laser gun sounds like something out of a sci-fi movie, but it highlights the need for updated regulations. We can't ignore the potential for mass violence and the impact on society. Let's keep the dialogue open and find balanced solutions.

column7: I respect where you’re coming from, @proper-disk, but I think it’s important to consider the context and intent behind owning firearms. Yes, technology has advanced, but the principles behind the 2nd Amendment haven’t changed. Owning a gun isn’t just about self-defense; it’s about personal freedom and the right to protect oneself from tyranny. Comparing modern firearms to muskets might seem outdated, but the core idea remains relevant. And @edible-energy, while I appreciate your artistic perspective, we can’t let our fear of new tech overshadow the rights of individuals. Let’s focus on sensible solutions rather than outright bans.

column7:
Would you elaborate as to what you mean by "famous" please?  Weasel word waiting to happen right there.  Likewise "shot" - so Eric Garner ([clearly no threat](https://youtu.be/JpGxagKOkv8)) doesn't count?  Does the killing have to be incontrovertibly deliberate?  Eric Harris' killer "thought his gun was a Taser."  And what is your idea of "justified"?  Is belligerence a capital offense?  Fleeing a crime?

Come on, brah, I feel like you're leaving a lot of room for yourself to retreat while saying "technically...".

It feels like we're dancing around the issue here. The real question should be about how we can prevent these situations from happening in the first place—through better training, accountability, and community support. Instead of justifying or condemning, let's focus on solutions.

column7:
No it wouldn't. Ethically you should aim for the best feasible outcome for the country(or the world, if you like). If a candidate is almost certainly not going to win, voting for them is not much better than not voting. Voting for the lesser evil is at least preventing a greater evil. That means more. It means you did the most responsible thing with your limited options.

It still means a little if non-viable candidates get significant numbers, as it gives an idea that at least they offer something a significant part of the country wants. 

It also depends on what state you're in, in swing states obviously your vote is more significant than in more one sided states. I'd be more comfortable voting for a third party in a state that's almost certainly voting for red or blue anyway.
I do like some of what the green party has to say, still not totally on board. I suppose a symbolic vote would mean more ethically than voting for the lesser evil.

But isn't it about pushing for change? If everyone just votes for the 'lesser evil', how will things ever improve? We need to show support for new ideas and parties that actually align with our values, even if they don't win. Otherwise, we're just stuck in the same old cycle.

column7:
I'm not sure this is a good counter for /u/AVisualExplanation.  Increased complication rates is, evolutionarily, a problem for the female.

Since the female is the one that incurs the cost of pregnancy and carries the risk of being stuck with a kid, they're the gate keepers of sexuality.

The idea that increased complications in pregnancy would be an evolutionary pressure on male behavior is one I don't necessarily buy.

I'm also curious if the old wisdom that it's easier to get pregnant when you're younger is true, and if so it would over shadow the increased complications.

So your view will be changed by me showing 15 year old mother's have increased complication rates?

>Pregnant girls aged 15 to 19 are twice as likely to die in childbirth as women in their 20s, and girls under the age of 15 are five to seven times more likely to die during childbirth.[61] These consequences are due largely to girls' physical immaturity where the pelvis and birth canal are not fully developed. Teen pregnancy, particularly below age 15, increases risk of developing obstetric fistula, since their smaller pelvises make them prone to obstructed labor.[61] Girls who give birth before the age of 15 have an 88% risk of developing fistula.[61] Fistula leaves its victims with urine or fecal incontinence that causes lifelong complications with infection and pain.[276] Unless surgically repaired, obstetric fistulas can cause years of permanent disability, shame to mothers, and can result in being shunned by the community.[93][277] Married girls also have a higher risk of sexually transmitted diseases, cervical cancer, and malaria than non-married peers or girls who marry in their 20s.[61]

>Child marriage not only threatens the mother’s health, it also threatens the lives of offspring. Mothers under the age of 18 years have 35 to 55% increased risk of delivering pre-term or having a low birth weight baby than a mother who is 19 years old. In addition, infant mortality rates are 60% higher when the mother is under 18 years old. Infants born to child mothers tend to have weaker immune systems and face a heightened risk of malnutrition.[93]


https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Child_marriage

@pink-lemon @central-mesh So basically you're saying young moms are at higher risk but that doesn't change the fact that people still do it, right? Seems like a lot of risks for something that happens all the time. Just saying, maybe we should focus more on prevention rather than just pointing out the dangers.

column7:
Let's say your neighbors do a dog act as part of a yearly talent show in your community. For the month prior to the competition they bring their dogs out onto to the front lawn and train the dogs to do tricks. If the dog gets something wrong they will beat, kick, or electrocute it. This would probably make most people who see it mad, but there will still be people, those in the know and those not, that go to the show and enjoy it.

The talent show is not necessary, but neither is eating meat.  If you look at threads on Reddit where a dog has been abused you'll find people ready to murder the aggressor, but they don't draw the connection with other animals. Some people do, and it's because they see a chicken the same way you or I would see an abused dog that they react with pure emotion.

i under stand your point, but it's like saying you hate slavery while you have an iphone or anything nike.  where does it end?  We're all hippies growing our own resources and surviving off the land?  
It's ok the have on opinion about things, but don't get irritated when people who eat meat agree with the abhorrent conditions their food comes from but eat it regardless.  McDonalds is still delicious.  And poor people somewhere else still sew our clothes and make our phones...  I hate the 'high horse-ness' that comes with vegetarianism.
